title: The Whale
logline: A morbidly obese English teacher attempts to reconnect with his estranged teenage daughter during the final week of his life while confronting his guilt over his former partner's suicide.
antagonistOrConflict: Charlie's self-destructive eating disorder and his guilt over Alan's death

actBreaks.description: Charlie's health crisis escalates and his relationship with Ellie begins, establishing the central dynamic that will drive the story forward.

validation.ruling: Liz discovers Ellie's essay on the counter and recognizes the name, fundamentally shifting the story from Charlie's medical crisis to the revelation of his estranged daughter's involvement - an irreversible plot development that cannot be undone.

actBreaks.description: Mary's arrival brings Charlie's past fully into the present, forcing him to confront the truth about Alan's death and his role in it.

validation.ruling: Mary confronts Ellie about the money Charlie offered her while Charlie is hooked to oxygen and visibly dying, forcing all secrets into the open and creating the final act's desperate emotional stakes where everyone's true motivations are exposed.

naturalStructure.structuralPersonality: This is a chamber piece that unfolds almost entirely within Charlie's claustrophobic apartment, using a classical three-act structure compressed into an intense week-long timeframe. The script operates like a pressure cooker, with each act introducing new characters who force Charlie deeper into confrontation with his past and mortality. Act 1 establishes Charlie's physical and emotional state through his interactions with Liz and Thomas, then introduces the central relationship with Ellie. Act 2 deepens these relationships while building toward Mary's entrance, which serves as the major complication. Act 3 strips away all pretense as Charlie faces his final hours, forcing brutal honesty from all characters. The structure mirrors Charlie's physical deterioration - each act represents a further stage of his decline while paradoxically moving him closer to emotional breakthrough.
naturalStructure.pacingNotes: The pacing is deliberately methodical in the first half, with many short scenes (scenes 6-14 average under 1 page each) that establish Charlie's daily routine and physical limitations. The script accelerates dramatically after Mary's entrance in scene 43, with longer, more emotionally intense scenes. The final act maintains breakneck emotional intensity while Charlie's physical condition deteriorates rapidly, culminating in the extended final scene between Charlie and Ellie.

incitingIncident.description: Charlie, a 600-pound man, masturbates to gay porn in his squalid apartment when he suddenly experiences severe chest pain and panic, reaching for a worn essay about Moby Dick before someone knocks at his door.

validation.ruling: Charlie's heart attack while masturbating shatters his ordinary world and forces him into irreversible stakes - he cannot ignore his mortality and must confront his isolation when Thomas unexpectedly arrives.

midpoint.description: Thomas shows Charlie religious pamphlets about the end times while Charlie questions whether Thomas is bothered by the idea of the world ending, with Thomas expressing his belief in a better world coming.

validation.ruling: Scene #29 lacks a clear shift from reactive to proactive behavior. Scene #31 shows Liz aggressively confronting Thomas about his missionary work and warning him away from Charlie, representing a proactive escalation that raises personal stakes and creates a point of no return in the relationships.

climax.description: Ellie discovers that Charlie submitted an essay she wrote in eighth grade about Moby Dick, initially furious until Charlie helps her recognize her own writing and remember how it made him feel saddest when reading the boring whale descriptions.

validation.ruling: Charlie reveals to Ellie that the essay is her own eighth-grade work about Moby Dick, creating the peak emotional confrontation where he demonstrates his love by showing he treasured her writing for years - this resolves the central father-daughter conflict and completes both character arcs.

scenes.heading: INT. - LIVING ROOM - DAY
scenes.summary: Charlie, a 600-pound man, masturbates to gay porn in his squalid apartment when he suddenly experiences severe chest pain and panic, reaching for a worn essay about Moby Dick before someone knocks at his door.
scenes.turningPointNote: Opening crisis establishes Charlie's physical condition and sets the story in motion

scenes.heading: INT. - LIVING ROOM - SHORTLY LATER
scenes.summary: Liz takes Charlie's blood pressure and examines him with a stethoscope while Thomas stands in the corner, revealing Charlie's dangerously high blood pressure and breathing problems.

scenes.heading: INT. - LIVING ROOM - LATE THAT NIGHT
scenes.summary: Charlie eats the last piece of chicken from a bucket while watching late-night TV, experiencing chest pain as he bends over and softly reciting lines about Moby Dick to himself.

scenes.heading: INT. - HALLWAY - SHORTLY LATER
scenes.summary: Charlie moves down the hallway on his walker, breathing heavily, and barely manages to fit through the bedroom doorway.

scenes.heading: INT. - BEDROOM - CONTINUOUS
scenes.summary: Charlie slowly lowers himself onto his soiled mattress surrounded by food containers and soda bottles, struggling to breathe before reaching for a soda and staring at the ceiling.

scenes.heading: EXT. - PALOUSE LANDSCAPE
scenes.summary: Shots of rolling Palouse hills with wind that begins to sound like ocean waves, with the title card 'TUESDAY' appearing.

scenes.heading: INT. - HALLWAY - MORNING
scenes.summary: Charlie makes his way down the hallway with great effort, notices a bird on the window sill, then continues toward the bathroom.

scenes.heading: INT. - BATHROOM - CONTINUOUS
scenes.summary: Charlie performs his morning routine of using mouthwash, struggling to remove his shirt, wiping himself down, brushing teeth, and applying deodorant.

scenes.heading: INT. - KITCHEN - MORNING
scenes.summary: Charlie opens a kitchen drawer containing bread loaves and discovers old candy bars he had forgotten about, examining one before putting it back.

scenes.heading: INT. - KITCHEN - MOMENTS LATER
scenes.summary: Charlie tears bread into small pieces for a plate while his eyes continually drift toward the drawer containing the hidden candy bars.

scenes.heading: INT. - KITCHEN - MOMENTS LATER
scenes.summary: Charlie opens the candy drawer, eats half a candy bar in two large bites, then hesitates while trying to put the remaining half back.

scenes.heading: INT. - KITCHEN - MOMENTS LATER
scenes.summary: Charlie stares at half a dozen empty candy bar wrappers on the counter, then hides them at the bottom of the trash can before grabbing the plate of bread crumbs.

scenes.heading: INT. - LIVING ROOM - MOMENTS LATER
scenes.summary: Charlie places the plate of bread crumbs on the window sill.

scenes.heading: INT. - LIVING ROOM - MOMENTS LATER
scenes.summary: Charlie collapses on his couch and searches Google for information about congestive heart failure and obesity, experiencing increasing anxiety and chest pain as he reads.

scenes.heading: INT. - LIVING ROOM - THAT AFTERNOON
scenes.summary: Ellie, Charlie's 17-year-old daughter, arrives and asks if she'll get fat, revealing she was suspended from school for sending a threatening text and expressing anger about Charlie's absence from her life.
scenes.turningPointNote: Charlie's estranged daughter enters the story, introducing the central relationship conflict

scenes.heading: INT. - LIVING ROOM - LATER THAT AFTERNOON
scenes.summary: Charlie sits on his laptop looking at Ellie's Facebook page, noting she has only 17 friends, when a pizza delivery arrives and he instructs the delivery boy to leave it at the door.

scenes.heading: INT. - FRONT DOORWAY - CONTINUOUS
scenes.summary: Charlie opens his door to find the pizza box on the floor and watches the delivery boy drive away from the parking lot.

scenes.heading: INT. - LIVING ROOM - THAT EVENING
scenes.summary: Liz uses a stress-measuring device on Charlie while unpacking groceries, discovering an empty ice cream container and confronting Charlie about his eating, leading to an argument about his self-destructive behavior.

scenes.heading: EXT. - PALOUSE LANDSCAPE
scenes.summary: The Palouse hills are shown with wind that now more distinctly resembles ocean waves, with the title card 'WEDNESDAY' appearing.

scenes.heading: INT. - BEDROOM - EARLY MORNING
scenes.summary: Charlie jolts awake from his phone alarm, covered in sweat with his heart pounding, staring at the ceiling while wheezing before turning off the alarm.

scenes.heading: INT. - KITCHEN - LATER THAT AFTERNOON
scenes.summary: Charlie searches for food in his kitchen, eating old Slimfast bars and pizza crusts before staring at a jar of mayonnaise.

scenes.heading: INT. - LIVING ROOM - LATER
scenes.summary: Charlie searches through old notebooks at his bookcase, finding a purple notebook and accidentally dropping papers that turn out to be an old essay on Faulkner.

scenes.heading: INT. - BEDROOM - SHORTLY LATER
scenes.summary: Charlie stares at an old box on a high shelf in his bedroom, reaching for it unsuccessfully and nearly falling before catching himself on his walker.

scenes.heading: INT. - BEDROOM - SHORTLY LATER
scenes.summary: Charlie uses a reaching claw to successfully grab the box from the shelf, pulling it down to the ground.

scenes.heading: INT. - BEDROOM - MOMENTS LATER
scenes.summary: Charlie collapses onto his mattress and opens the box, revealing framed photos, a Bible, and trinkets including a seashell, becoming upset and holding back tears as he stares at the contents.

scenes.heading: INT. - LIVING ROOM - LATER
scenes.summary: Charlie reads a student's essay about Walt Whitman aloud while Ellie types on her phone, leading to an argument when Charlie tries to correct her interpretation and Ellie storms off to the bathroom.

scenes.heading: INT. - BATHROOM - CONTINUOUS
scenes.summary: Charlie barely fits through the bathroom door and sits on the toilet using cinder blocks for support, looking at himself in the mirror and fighting back tears.

scenes.heading: INT. - LIVING ROOM - CONTINUOUS
scenes.summary: Ellie finds Charlie's notebook and notices the bird at the window when Thomas arrives at the door, leading to a tense conversation where Ellie reveals she's Charlie's daughter and makes crude comments about his sexuality.

scenes.heading: INT. - LIVING ROOM - LATER
scenes.summary: Thomas shows Charlie religious pamphlets about the end times while Charlie questions whether Thomas is bothered by the idea of the world ending, with Thomas expressing his belief in a better world coming.

scenes.heading: INT. - BEDROOM - CONTINUOUS
scenes.summary: Charlie enters his bedroom in a wheelchair, looking at the sweat outline on his mattress before wheeling to the window overlooking wheat fields and opening it to lean outside.

scenes.heading: INT. - LIVING ROOM - MOMENTS LATER
scenes.summary: Liz interrogates Thomas about his background while smoking, becoming hostile when he reveals he's doing missionary work and warning him to stay away from Charlie.

scenes.heading: INT. - LIVING ROOM - THAT NIGHT
scenes.summary: Charlie makes sandwiches in his wheelchair when chest pain causes him to drop them, leading to a moment of rage where he throws a book and shatters a lamp.

scenes.heading: INT. - LIVING ROOM - LATER THAT NIGHT
scenes.summary: Charlie orders pizza delivery and has a brief conversation with Danny the delivery boy, who introduces himself through the door.

scenes.heading: INT. - LIVING ROOM - LATER
scenes.summary: Charlie finds Ellie's notebook and reads her entries: 'This apartment smells. This notebook is retarded. I hate everyone,' which makes him smile broadly.

scenes.heading: EXT. - PALOUSE LANDSCAPE
scenes.summary: The Palouse hills now distinctly resemble aggressive ocean waves with the title card 'THURSDAY' appearing.

scenes.heading: INT. - LIVING ROOM - THE NEXT DAY
scenes.summary: Charlie teaches his online class, reading a student's dismissive post about thesis strategies and expressing frustration about his students' lack of genuine reactions to literature.

scenes.heading: INT. - LIVING ROOM - LATER
scenes.summary: Ellie arrives asking for her homework while Charlie tries to get her to write more in her notebook, leading to Ellie bluntly stating she hates him before reluctantly agreeing to write.

scenes.heading: INT. - LIVING ROOM - LATER
scenes.summary: Ellie drugs Charlie's sandwich with ground Ambien while he sleeps, then smokes marijuana when Thomas arrives and discovers what she's done.

scenes.heading: INT. - BEDROOM - CONTINUOUS
scenes.summary: Thomas stands in Charlie's bedroom looking around while Ellie remains outside the locked door.

scenes.heading: INT. - HALLWAY - CONTINUOUS
scenes.summary: Ellie listens at the bedroom door and secretly records Thomas on her phone as he explains why he left home.

scenes.heading: INT. - BEDROOM - CONTINUOUS
scenes.summary: Thomas confesses through the door about being caught smoking pot and betrayed by his best friend, while wandering around Charlie's bedroom and discovering the open box.

scenes.heading: INT. - HALLWAY - CONTINUOUS
scenes.summary: Thomas unlocks the bedroom door and emerges to find Ellie, who takes his picture, just as Liz and Mary (Ellie's mother) arrive.

scenes.heading: INT. - LIVING ROOM - SHORTLY LATER
scenes.summary: Liz hooks Charlie up to oxygen while Mary smokes and watches him, leading to a confrontation where Mary reveals Charlie abandoned his family for Alan, and Ellie explodes in anger before leaving with Mary.
scenes.turningPointNote: Major revelation about Charlie's past and Alan's death, with family confrontation reaching breaking point

scenes.heading: INT. - LIVING ROOM - THAT NIGHT
scenes.summary: Charlie looks at Ellie's Facebook page and sees she posted a photo of the dead bird crushed under the plate, while Danny the delivery boy checks on him through the door.

scenes.heading: INT. - LIVING ROOM - MOMENTS LATER
scenes.summary: Charlie ravenously devours pizza, eating furiously and angrily while struggling to breathe, with sweat pouring down his face.

scenes.heading: INT. - LIVING ROOM - SHORTLY LATER
scenes.summary: While continuing to eat pizza, Charlie writes an angry post to all his students containing profanity and the phrase 'THIS CLASS IS FUCKING BULLSHIT.'

scenes.heading: INT. - KITCHEN - SHORTLY LATER
scenes.summary: Charlie binges on food from his kitchen including mayonnaise eaten with his bare hands, cereal bars, and junk food until chest pain forces him to stop.

scenes.heading: INT. - KITCHEN - LATER THAT NIGHT
scenes.summary: Charlie falls asleep in his wheelchair and is awakened by Thomas knocking at the door asking to come inside.

scenes.heading: INT. - LIVING ROOM - CONTINUOUS
scenes.summary: Thomas arrives disheveled and reveals that Ellie sent the recording and photos to his parents' church, but surprisingly his parents responded with love and want him to come home, leading Thomas to question everything he believed.
scenes.turningPointNote: Thomas's crisis of faith and revelation about his parents' true feelings creates a major character turning point

scenes.heading: EXT. - PALOUSE LANDSCAPE
scenes.summary: The Palouse hills appear distorted and aggressive, fully transformed into a stormy ocean with loud wave sounds, as the title card 'FRIDAY' appears.

scenes.heading: INT. - BEDROOM - DAWN
scenes.summary: Charlie sits in his wheelchair at dawn, retrieves a cardboard box of Alan's belongings using his reaching claw, and pulls out a framed photo of Alan at his church confirmation.

scenes.heading: INT. - LIVING ROOM - MOMENTS LATER
scenes.summary: Charlie places Alan's confirmation photo next to an existing photo of himself and Alan, then arranges other items from the box including a seashell and Bible on the shelf.

scenes.heading: INT. - LIVING ROOM - AFTERNOON
scenes.summary: Charlie, sweating and dazed, records a final message to his online students about being replaced and reads aloud from their honest, unrewritten essays that make him smile.

scenes.heading: INT. - LIVING ROOM - LATER
scenes.summary: Liz arrives to find Charlie's broken computer, angrily tells him she hates him for making her relive Alan's death, describes finding Alan's body curled up like a fetus in Charlie's bed, then leaves sandwiches and her phone before exiting.
scenes.turningPointNote: Liz's devastating revelation about finding Alan's body and her decision to leave Charlie her phone marks her final break from him

scenes.heading: EXT. - APARTMENT BUILDING STAIRS - CONTINUOUS
scenes.summary: Liz stands outside Charlie's apartment holding the phone, considers calling for help, then puts it away and sits on the stairs crying.

scenes.heading: INT. - LIVING ROOM - CONTINUOUS
scenes.summary: Ellie discovers that Charlie submitted an essay she wrote in eighth grade about Moby Dick, initially furious until Charlie helps her recognize her own writing and remember how it made him feel saddest when reading the boring whale descriptions.
scenes.turningPointNote: The revelation that Charlie has kept Ellie's eighth grade essay creates a profound emotional breakthrough between father and daughter
